excerpts: The map works by using a web scraping tool that scans copies of tickets uploaded to the city's payment portal website, and looks for them by guessing the new ID number for tickets issued on a given day. By scanning the website every few seconds, it can see where tickets are being written in close to real time.

The app, which launched Tuesday, allows people to see the location of parking cops in close to real time. It even includes a leaderboard showing which officers made the city the most money that week: Officer 0336 racked up more than $15,000 before the app was rendered useless.

Walz, who admits he’s not “pro or anti parking cop,” got the idea after seeing violation notices for graffiti tacked to the side of buildings. He saw that the notices had a complaint ID and that the way complaint numbers were assigned followed predictable patterns and were published online by the city.

Walz had already spotted some interesting patterns in the data. One officer sits by the Hyatt Regency near the Ferry Building, writing tickets against cars illegally parked at the white curb behind the hotel.

excerpts: The app, which launched Tuesday, allows people to see the location of parking cops in close to real time. It even includes a leaderboard showing which officers made the city the most money that week: Officer 0336 racked up more than $15,000 before the app was rendered useless.

Walz, who admits he’s not “pro or anti parking cop,” got the idea after seeing violation notices for graffiti tacked to the side of buildings. He saw that the notices had a complaint ID and that the way complaint numbers were assigned followed predictable patterns and were published online by the city.

Walz had already spotted some interesting patterns in the data. One officer sits by the Hyatt Regency near the Ferry Building, writing tickets against cars illegally parked at the white curb behind the hotel.

The map works by using a web scraping tool that scans copies of tickets uploaded to the city's payment portal website, and looks for them by guessing the new ID number for tickets issued on a given day. By scanning the website every few seconds, it can see where tickets are being written in close to real time.
citations.title: Viral parking ticket app lasts just 4 hours as city kills project

content.executive_summary: Riley Walz's creative philosophy is rooted in a form of playful engineering that uses technology to reveal hidden cultural patterns and evoke digital nostalgia. His taste and aesthetic are defined by a preference for simple, single-purpose, and often ephemeral web experiments over polished, commercial products. A central tenet of his work is the exploitation of 'platform quirks'—leveraging predictable but overlooked features of systems like YouTube's default filenames (IMG_0001), municipal payment portals (Find My Parking Cops), or commercial APIs like Shazam (Bop Spotter). This approach allows him to create projects that function as cultural time capsules or real-time data visualizations of urban life. His aesthetic often incorporates a retro or minimal feel, mimicking familiar interfaces like Gmail (Jmail) to make complex data accessible, or using simple, low-friction UIs that prioritize the core concept. Humor, irony, and a prankster sensibility are key elements, framing potentially serious topics like surveillance as playful 'culture surveillance' aimed at 'catching vibes' rather than criminals. Ultimately, Walz's work celebrates curiosity, rapid prototyping, and the joy of discovering and sharing interesting things found in the digital and physical world.

key_preferences_and_rejections.description: A core methodological pattern is the exploitation of 'platform quirks'—small, predictable, or overlooked characteristics of digital platforms and public datasets. He leverages these quirks (e.g., default file names, sequential IDs, public APIs) to bootstrap projects quickly and reveal hidden cultural or infrastructural patterns.
key_preferences_and_rejections.supporting_examples: IMG_0001 (used default 'IMG_XXXX' YouTube filenames), Find My Parking Cops (scraped predictable municipal citation IDs), Bop Spotter (used the Shazam API as a cultural sensor), Amtrak Right Now (inferred passenger counts from reservation inventory data).

key_preferences_and_rejections.description: He shows a strong affinity for old-internet nostalgia and authenticity, creating projects that evoke the feeling of an earlier, less-curated web. This involves surfacing raw, unedited, and low-production content that feels like a 'time capsule'.
key_preferences_and_rejections.supporting_examples: IMG_0001 is the primary example, designed to shuffle through millions of early-iPhone YouTube uploads to find 'unedited, pure moments from random lives.' Walz described it as creating a feeling of 'memories' and a 'gut punch' of nostalgia.

key_preferences_and_rejections.description: Humor, irony, and a 'prankster' energy are central to his work. This is evident in project concepts, witty or pun-based names, and real-world stunts that playfully critique or disrupt systems.
key_preferences_and_rejections.supporting_examples: Mehran’s Steak House (a one-night fake restaurant), the Waymo prank (a 'DDOS' on self-driving cars), the fake 'Andrew Walz' political candidate, and the name 'Bop Spotter' (a pun on the surveillance tool 'ShotSpotter').

key_preferences_and_rejections.description: He demonstrates a sustained interest in making urban systems, public transit, and municipal infrastructure more legible. His projects often deconstruct and visualize the operational data of these systems.
key_preferences_and_rejections.supporting_examples: Find My Parking Cops (visualized parking enforcement routes), Amtrak Right Now (visualized passenger loads), and his GitHub repositories 'dresden' (a transit bot for Dresden, Germany) and 'osm-public-transit' (a collection of OpenStreetMap tools).

key_preferences_and_rejections.description: His projects favor minimal, legible, and often familiar UI metaphors. The goal is to make experimental concepts immediately accessible and reduce friction for the user, sometimes by mimicking well-known applications.
key_preferences_and_rejections.supporting_examples: Jmail presents public documents in a mimicked Gmail interface. IMG_0001 uses a simple shuffle player and a 'TV remote' metaphor. Bop Spotter uses a simple, continuous live feed.

key_preferences_and_rejections.description: He recontextualizes surveillance aesthetics as a tool for playful 'culture surveillance.' Rather than for punitive enforcement, he uses data collection to document and observe public behavior, tastes, and cultural trends, often with an ironic framing.
key_preferences_and_rejections.supporting_examples: Bop Spotter is explicitly described as 'culture surveillance' for 'catching vibes, not criminals.' Other projects like Library Spy (inferring book checkouts) and Looksmapping (ranking restaurants by reviewer 'hotness') also fit this theme.

key_preferences_and_rejections.description: He explicitly and consistently rejects pretentious labels, particularly 'artist.' He prefers to identify as an 'engineer' or simply a 'guy who knows something about technology,' framing his work as technical experimentation rather than fine art.
key_preferences_and_rejections.supporting_examples: In a WIRED interview, he stated, 'Just engineer is fine.' In The New Yorker, he was quoted as finding the word 'art' to be 'too pretentious' and said, 'I don't really like labels like that.'

key_preferences_and_rejections.description: He generally avoids heavy, productized scaling as the primary goal for his projects. The majority are conceived as lightweight, single-serving experiments, 'weekend builds,' or one-off stunts, prioritizing the concept and rapid execution over long-term commercialization.
key_preferences_and_rejections.supporting_examples: Many of his projects are ephemeral or experimental by nature. Find My Parking Cops was disabled within hours. The Waymo prank and Mehran's Steak House were one-time events. Routeshuffle, which has a subscription model, is a notable exception rather than the rule.

interest_in_old_internet_nostalgia.theme_description: Riley Walz demonstrates a profound fascination with the authentic, unedited, and less-curated nature of the early internet, particularly the Web 2.0 era. His work expresses a yearning for a bygone digital period characterized by raw, low-production content created for personal expression rather than polished, algorithmic performance. This theme is evident in his effort to surface what he calls 'unedited, pure moments from random lives.' Media profiles note that he creates projects that feel like 'time machines' or deliver a 'gut punch' of nostalgia, acting as a 'portal into another era when people just wanted to make something fun.' He is drawn to the stories found in unexpected digital corners, like low-view YouTube videos, which represent a form of cultural memory before the internet became heavily commercialized and curated.
interest_in_old_internet_nostalgia.key_project_example: The primary project exemplifying this theme is IMG_0001. For this project, Walz created a bot that crawled YouTube to find approximately 5 million videos uploaded between 2009 and 2012 that retained their default iPhone filenames, such as 'IMG_0001.MOV'. The project's website presents these videos through a simple shuffle player with an interface resembling a television screen and a remote control. By surfacing these raw, often personal and low-view-count clips, IMG_0001 functions as an interactive archive of a specific period of internet history, showcasing authentic moments captured before the rise of influencer culture.
interest_in_old_internet_nostalgia.core_concepts: The underlying values Walz explores through this theme are authenticity, digital archaeology, and the creation of 'time capsules.' He is interested in the 'hidden deposits' of data that tell stories about our recent past. His work in this area is a form of digital archaeology, unearthing and preserving cultural artifacts that would otherwise be lost in the noise of the modern internet. By creating what he calls 'time machines,' he aims to capture and re-present the feeling of a 'different era,' valuing the unedited and pure quality of early user-generated content as a more genuine reflection of human experience.

fascination_with_platform_quirks.methodology_description: A core method central to nearly all of Riley Walz's work is the identification and exploitation of 'platform quirks'—small, predictable, and often overlooked characteristics of digital platforms, public datasets, or APIs. His methodology involves treating these systems not just as tools but as readable environments that contain hidden data or behavioral signals. He demonstrates a consistent pattern of finding a stable, machine-readable artifact (like a naming convention or a sequential ID) and building a lightweight experiment around it to make an otherwise invisible pattern visible. This approach allows him to create novel cultural, visual, or utilitarian projects by repurposing existing infrastructure in unintended ways, effectively turning the platforms' own logic into a medium for his experiments.
fascination_with_platform_quirks.common_techniques: Walz repeatedly employs a set of common techniques to leverage platform quirks. These include: data scraping, where he develops crawlers and parsers to systematically extract information from public web pages; API bootstrapping, where he utilizes official or undocumented APIs to gather data, as seen with his transit projects and Bop Spotter; and reverse engineering, where he deduces the logic of a system, such as the predictable, sequential citation ID patterns in San Francisco's municipal payment portal for his 'Find My Parking Cops' project. Many of his projects are executed as rapid 'weekend builds,' emphasizing quick public deployment to test a concept and generate discussion.
fascination_with_platform_quirks.examples_of_quirks:
Specific examples of platform quirks Walz has exploited include:
- **YouTube's Default Filenames:** For the IMG_0001 project, he leveraged the fact that early iPhone uploads to YouTube often retained their default 'IMG_XXXX' filenames, creating a searchable corpus of nostalgic videos.
- **Predictable Municipal Citation IDs:** For 'Find My Parking Cops,' he reverse-engineered the SFMTA's payment portal, which used predictable, sequential citation IDs, allowing him to infer the near-real-time locations of parking enforcement officers.
- **Shazam's API:** In 'Bop Spotter,' he uses a microphone and an old phone to continuously feed ambient street audio into Shazam's song recognition service, turning a consumer music app into a tool for 'culture surveillance.'
- **Amtrak's Reservation Inventory:** For 'Amtrak Right Now,' he infers the number of passengers on trains by probing the reservation/ticketing API to see how many seats are available versus the total capacity.
- **NYPL Book Availability Counters:** In 'Library Spy,' he scrapes the public catalog of a library branch, using the decrease in the number of available copies of a book as a proxy for a checkout event.
- **Google Maps Reviewer Photos:** For 'Looksmapping,' he scraped millions of Google Maps reviews and used the publicly available profile photos of reviewers as inputs for a facial attractiveness AI model.

engagement_with_urban_systems_and_transi.focus_areas: Riley Walz has a sustained and recurring interest in engaging with urban systems, with a particular focus on public transit, mapping data, and municipal infrastructure. His work interrogates the digital layers of city life, treating urban environments as readable systems that can be deconstructed, visualized, and reinterpreted through data. He explores the operational behaviors of public agencies (like parking enforcement), the flow of people through transit networks (like Amtrak), and the cultural pulse of a neighborhood (like the music in the Mission District). His focus is often on making opaque systems more legible to the public through playful or provocative data visualizations and experiments.
engagement_with_urban_systems_and_transi.key_projects:
A significant portion of Walz's portfolio is dedicated to this theme, including:
- **Find My Parking Cops:** A short-lived but viral project that inferred the near-real-time locations of San Francisco parking enforcement officers by scraping the city's citation payment portal.
- **Amtrak Right Now (Amtrak-trains):** A live website that visualizes the inferred number of passengers currently on board Amtrak trains across the United States, based on reservation inventory data.
- **'dresden' bot:** A Telegram bot created for the DVB and VVO transit network in Dresden, Germany, which provides real-time departure and trip-planning information by querying the transit authority's mobile API.
- **'osm-public-transit':** A GitHub repository curating a list of tools and endpoints for using OpenStreetMap data to enhance public transportation mapping and analysis.
- **Waymo Prank ('WAYMO DDOS'):** A social stunt where he organized 50 people to simultaneously hail Waymo self-driving cars to a dead-end street, testing the limits of the autonomous vehicle system.
- **Bop Spotter:** An installation in San Francisco's Mission District that captures the ambient musical landscape of the neighborhood, functioning as a form of urban 'culture surveillance.'
engagement_with_urban_systems_and_transi.data_sources_used:
To build these projects, Walz utilizes a variety of common data sources, often in creative ways. These include:
- **Municipal Portals and APIs:** He scrapes or reverse-engineers public-facing city websites, such as the SFMTA parking ticket portal, or uses mobile APIs from transit agencies like Dresden's DVB.
- **OpenStreetMap (OSM) and Protomaps:** He frequently uses OpenStreetMap for geographic and transit route data and Protomaps for creating basemaps for his visualizations.
- **Reservation and Ticketing APIs:** He probes reservation inventory systems, like Amtrak's, to infer real-time occupancy data.
- **GTFS (General Transit Feed Specification):** While he sometimes uses direct API scraping, his work and curated tool lists (like 'osm-public-transit') show a deep engagement with standard transit data formats like GTFS for understanding and visualizing transit networks.

approach_to_surveillance_aesthetics.central_concept: Riley Walz's core idea regarding surveillance is what he terms 'culture surveillance.' This concept involves repurposing monitoring tools and public data not for punitive enforcement or security, but for playful, ambient observation of cultural trends. Instead of tracking individuals for infractions, his projects aim to capture and document collective public behaviors and tastes, such as the music being played in a neighborhood. This approach is rooted in a curiosity about the hidden data layers of public life and a desire to make them visible and legible in an engaging, often nostalgic or humorous, way. His work in this area serves as a commentary on the ubiquity of surveillance technologies by inverting their typical purpose from control to cultural documentation.
approach_to_surveillance_aesthetics.primary_example: The main project illustrating 'culture surveillance' is Bop Spotter. This installation consists of a solar-powered Android phone with a microphone, placed in San Francisco's Mission District. It continuously records ambient audio, uploads it in 10-minute segments, and processes short clips through the Shazam service to identify songs being played on the street. The identified tracks are then published to a public, real-time feed. Walz describes it as a 'living time capsule' of the neighborhood's musical taste. The project uses the mechanics of surveillance—a persistent, non-consensual audio recording device in a public space—but its output is a cultural artifact: a playlist of 'vibes' rather than a log of activities or conversations.
approach_to_surveillance_aesthetics.ethical_framing: Walz frames the ethical implications of his surveillance projects by drawing a sharp contrast between punitive and playful observation. His key distinction is summarized in the phrase 'catching vibes' versus 'catching criminals.' He openly acknowledges the lack of consent inherent in projects like Bop Spotter, stating on the project's page, 'No one notices, no one consents.' However, he immediately pivots to reframe the project's intent as non-threatening and culturally focused. By positioning the work as an act of documenting culture rather than policing behavior, he attempts to sidestep the more sinister connotations of surveillance, arguing that the purpose is observation and archival, not control or punishment.
approach_to_surveillance_aesthetics.key_quote: This is culture surveillance. No one notices, no one consents. But it's not about catching criminals. It's about catching vibes.

use_of_humor_and_pranks.role_of_humor: Humor and irony are central to Riley Walz's work, serving as a primary vehicle for engagement and critique. He employs a deadpan, prankster energy to highlight the absurdities and hidden mechanics of technological and social systems. The function of humor is often to make a technical or conceptual point more accessible and shareable, turning what could be a dry data analysis into a viral event. For example, by framing a surveillance project with a pun ('Bop Spotter'), he invites a playful interpretation while still provoking a discussion about public monitoring. His humor is often rooted in curiosity and a 'just to see if I could' attitude, allowing him to experiment with platform rules and social norms in a way that is both amusing and revealing.
use_of_humor_and_pranks.types_of_pranks: Walz's pranks manifest in several distinct forms. First are social engineering stunts, where he manipulates social or digital platforms to test their limits, such as creating a fake political candidate ('Andrew Walz') who briefly achieved verification on Twitter. Second are digital provocations, which involve creating websites or tools that use data in unexpected or subversive ways, like the 'Jmail' project that presented the Epstein files in a mock Gmail interface. Third are physical or real-world stunts, which involve coordinating people to create a spectacle that tests the limits of a system, such as the 'Waymo DDOS' where dozens of people summoned self-driving cars to a dead-end street to create a traffic jam. Finally, he engages in elaborate fake pop-ups, like 'Mehran's Steak House,' a one-night-only fake restaurant in Manhattan designed as a social experiment.
use_of_humor_and_pranks.key_examples:
Some of Riley Walz's most notable pranks include:
- **Mehran’s Steak House (2023):** A one-night-only fake, high-end steakhouse pop-up in Manhattan, created with friends as a social experiment that garnered significant media attention.
- **Fake 'Andrew Walz' Candidate (2020):** As a teenager, Walz created a fake political candidate on Twitter and successfully got the account verified, exposing vulnerabilities in the platform's verification process.
- **Waymo 'DDOS' Stunt (2025):** Walz organized a stunt where approximately 50 people simultaneously summoned Waymo self-driving cars to the same dead-end street in San Francisco at dusk, creating a traffic jam of autonomous vehicles to test the system's response to an unusual surge in demand.
- **Find My Parking Cops (2025):** While also a data visualization project, its provocative framing and the act of exposing the near-real-time locations of enforcement officers carried a strong prankster energy, leading to its rapid shutdown by the city.

ui_ux_design_philosophy.core_principles: Riley Walz's design philosophy is fundamentally rooted in simplicity, legibility, and minimal user friction. His primary goal is to make his experiments immediately understandable and accessible to a broad audience, often by surfacing hidden or overlooked data in a clear, direct manner. He prioritizes direct functionality and purpose, creating single-purpose pages or portals that guide users to their goal without unnecessary embellishments or complex navigation. This approach is evident in projects designed to be 'immediately legible,' ensuring that the core concept is conveyed with minimal cognitive load. His work often acts as a 'portal,' making complex data or cultural phenomena accessible through straightforward interfaces, reflecting a belief in low-friction interaction and the power of making information visible.
ui_ux_design_philosophy.common_patterns: A recurring pattern in Walz's work is the mimicry of well-known applications to provide users with an instantly familiar interaction model. The most prominent example is the 'Jmail / J*' suite of projects, which adopts a Gmail-style interface to present public documents (like the Epstein files) as if they were in a personal inbox, thereby reducing the learning curve and focusing attention on the content. Another common pattern is the use of nostalgic interfaces to enhance the theme of a project. For IMG_0001, which surfaces old YouTube videos, the interface is designed to resemble a television screen, complete with a clickable image of a TV remote for navigation. This not only reinforces the project's nostalgic feel but also provides an intuitive interaction method. Other patterns include simple shuffle players, map-based data visualizations (as in 'Find My Parking Cops'), and live data feeds or single-stream visualizations.
ui_ux_design_philosophy.technical_approach: Walz's technical approach prioritizes function, resourcefulness, and rapid deployment over visual flair or heavy frameworks. He favors lightweight front-end technologies, as seen in the use of JavaScript, CSS, and Pug for his 'Musical Roulette' project, rather than complex, heavy frameworks. A core part of his methodology is bootstrapping projects by relying on external APIs (like Shazam for 'Bop Spotter'), data scraping, and exploiting 'platform quirks'—such as predictable filename patterns or municipal portal ID sequences. This allows for rapid prototyping, with many projects described as 'weekend builds.' His resourcefulness extends to hardware; for 'Bop Spotter,' he used a 'crappy Android phone,' a cardioid microphone, and a solar panel, demonstrating a preference for low-cost, DIY solutions that are functional enough to achieve the project's experimental goals.

project_naming_conventions.styles: Riley Walz employs several distinct styles for his project names, which often signal the project's intent and tone. One common style is the use of puns and cultural references. These names cleverly play on existing terms or services to create a memorable and witty title. A second style is the use of direct, descriptive titles that clearly and simply communicate the project's function or the data it uses. This approach is straightforward and leaves little room for ambiguity. A third style involves ironic or contextual naming, where the name's meaning is derived from the specific, often provocative, context of the project's content.
project_naming_conventions.tone: The typical tone conveyed by Walz's project names is humorous, ironic, and often deadpan. The names are frequently witty and playful, reflecting the experimental and sometimes prankster-like nature of his work. By riffing on the language of surveillance or popular utility apps, he creates an ironic juxtaposition that can be both amusing and thought-provoking. The tone is rarely overtly sarcastic, instead relying on a clever, understated humor that allows the audience to appreciate the joke or concept behind the project. This deadpan delivery makes the underlying social or technical commentary more impactful.
project_naming_conventions.examples: Specific examples clearly illustrate his naming conventions. For puns and cultural references, 'Bop Spotter' is a direct pun on the controversial gunshot detection system 'ShotSpotter,' reframing surveillance as 'catching vibes.' 'Find My Parking Cops' is a referential name that mimics Apple's 'Find My' applications to describe its function. For direct and descriptive titles, 'IMG_0001' directly references the default filename convention of early iPhone videos, which is the core of the project's dataset. 'Routeshuffle' is another example of a functional name that describes its purpose. For ironic and contextual naming, the 'Jmail / J*' projects use the 'J' prefix as a clear, provocative reference to Jeffrey Epstein, framing the presentation of public documents within a familiar but unsettling context.

stance_on_labels_art_vs_engineering.reasoning: He has stated that he finds the word 'art' to be 'too pretentious'. He elaborates on this by positioning himself not as an artist, but as 'just a guy who knows something about technology who just wants to see something cool, and I just share it with people to see what they think.' This reflects his focus on the technical and experimental aspects of his work over any perceived artistic intent.

avoidance_of_commercialization.primary_focus: Riley Walz's primary focus is on conceptual experimentation, curiosity-driven exploration, and rapid prototyping. His work is consistently characterized by the immediate execution of an idea to 'see what happens' rather than on building scalable, long-term, commercial products. The goal is often the act of discovery and sharing, or the playful provocation itself, not market viability or user growth.
avoidance_of_commercialization.project_characteristics: The vast majority of his projects are single-serving, experimental, or ephemeral by design. They often exist as simple websites, temporary installations, or short-lived stunts. Many are described as 'weekend builds' and are designed to demonstrate a single concept or exploit a specific platform quirk. Projects like 'Find My Parking Cops' were inherently fragile and were disabled within hours, a characteristic that seems to be an accepted part of his creative process.
avoidance_of_commercialization.notable_exceptions: While most of his work avoids commercialization, 'Routeshuffle', a random route generator, stands out as a notable exception. It is described as one of his few productized efforts and reportedly has a subscription widget for which approximately a hundred people pay. This indicates that while his primary interest is not commercial, he is capable of and has engaged in productization on a small scale.

development_methodology.cadence: Walz's development cadence is characterized by rapid prototyping and quick public deployment. Many of his projects are described as 'weekend builds' or being 'thrown together over a couple Sundays'. This fast-paced, iterative approach allows him to quickly test concepts, capitalize on timely events, and share his findings with the public without getting bogged down in long development cycles.
development_methodology.technical_approach: His technical approach is defined by resourcefulness and a reliance on lightweight, accessible web technologies. He consistently leverages APIs (like Shazam for Bop Spotter or transit APIs for 'dresden'), data scraping, and the exploitation of predictable patterns in public data (like YouTube filenames for IMG_0001 or municipal citation IDs for Find My Parking Cops). His projects are often bootstrapped quickly using standard web stacks (JavaScript, CSS) and open-source tools like OpenStreetMap, rather than relying on heavy, bespoke frameworks.
development_methodology.hardware_use: For his physical projects, Walz employs a low-cost, DIY approach using consumer-grade or repurposed hardware. The most prominent example is Bop Spotter, which was built for about $100 using an old 'crappy Android phone', a cardioid microphone, and a solar panel. This demonstrates a pragmatic focus on functionality and cost-effectiveness over high-end, custom-built equipment, reinforcing the experimental and accessible nature of his work.

ethical_and_societal_boundaries.areas_of_tension: Riley Walz's work consistently explores ethical gray areas, primarily revolving around privacy, consent, and the creative repurposing of public data. A central tension is his practice of capturing and analyzing data about people without their explicit consent, as articulated in his 'no one consents' statement for Bop Spotter. This raises questions about bystander privacy, as ambient audio or public data streams can inadvertently capture personal information. Other projects, like Library Spy (inferring book checkouts) and Looksmapping (ranking restaurants by reviewers' AI-rated attractiveness), push into more sensitive areas of behavioral inference and algorithmic bias. His work forces a confrontation with the question of what expectations of privacy are reasonable in public spaces and what ethical obligations a creator has when making public data newly legible and searchable.
ethical_and_societal_boundaries.documented_responses: Several of Walz's projects have elicited direct responses from public or corporate entities when they crossed a perceived boundary. The most prominent example is 'Find My Parking Cops,' a project that inferred the near-real-time locations of San Francisco parking enforcement officers. Within approximately four hours of its launch, the San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency (SFMTA) altered its website to disable the data source Walz was using, effectively shutting the project down. The agency cited staff safety and privacy concerns. Similarly, his 'Waymo DDOS' stunt prompted Waymo to limit pickups in the affected area. His earlier fake political candidate project led to scrutiny of Twitter's verification processes. These incidents demonstrate that when his experiments create tangible operational disruptions or safety concerns, institutions respond swiftly to mitigate the impact.
ethical_and_societal_boundaries.observed_boundaries: Walz's work tests the boundary between playful provocation and real-world impact. The observed line appears to be drawn at the point where a project transitions from a cultural or artistic statement to a tool with tangible, disruptive, or potentially harmful operational consequences. For instance, Bop Spotter, framed as 'culture surveillance,' has been allowed to operate, as its impact is largely seen as archival and cultural. In contrast, 'Find My Parking Cops' was immediately shut down because it provided a means to track and potentially interfere with the duties of city employees in near-real time. This suggests that while society and institutions may tolerate playful experiments that reveal data patterns, they are far less permissive when those experiments directly impact physical operations, safety, or create a tool for direct interference with civic functions.

A solar-powered microphone installed in San Francisco's Mission District that uses Shazam to continuously identify and publish a live feed of music playing on the street. Walz frames it as 'culture surveillance.'
A map project that reverse-engineered San Francisco's parking ticket payment portal to show the near-real-time locations of parking enforcement officers by scraping predictable citation IDs.

A series of browser-based archives, created with Luke Igel, that present publicly released documents (such as the Epstein files) within a mimicked Gmail interface to make them more browsable and accessible.

A fake, ludicrously high-end steakhouse pop-up run for a single night in Manhattan as a social experiment and prank, organized with friends.
A social media stunt where Walz created a fake political candidate and successfully got the account briefly verified on Twitter to test and expose weaknesses in the platform's verification process.
A live website that infers and visualizes the number of passengers currently on Amtrak trains across the U.S. by analyzing public reservation inventory data.
